│ ▲ Detected TypeScript < 4.9 or incompatible tooling, populating with │ JavaScript examples │ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 0.0.0-pr-34612-sha-d8de7419 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF3 | │ | Addons | @chromatic-com/storybook, @storybook/addon-vitest, │ @storybook/addon-a11y, @storybook/addon-docs, │ @storybook/addon-onboarding | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=js&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories.md...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/decorat...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/args.md?rende...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-stories/pla...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=jshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/writing-tests/v...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'

     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'

     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→

    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {

    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,

    69→}

1→import { useRef, useEffect } from 'react'
     2→import * as React from 'react'
     3→import { createPortal } from 'react-dom'
     4→
     5→type Props = {
     6→  selector: string
     7→}
     8→
     9→export const Portal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<Props>> = ({ children, selector }) => {
    10→  const ref = useRef<Element | null>(null)
    11→
    12→  useEffect(() => {
    13→    const element = document.querySelector<Element>(selector)
    14→    if (element) {
    15→      ref.current = element
    16→    }
    17→  }, [selector])
    18→
    19→  return ref.current ? createPortal(children, ref.current) : null
    20→}
    21→

1→import { createSlice, CaseReducer, PayloadAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'
     2→
     3→import { CartItem } from '../cart'
     4→
     5→export type OrderState = {
     6→  items: CartItem[]
     7→}
     8→
     9→const saveOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState, PayloadAction<CartItem[]>> = (state, action) => {
    10→  state.items = action.payload
    11→}
    12→
    13→const clearOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState> = (state) => {
    14→  state.items = []
    15→}
    16→
    17→const initialState: OrderState = {
    18→  items: [],
    19→}
    20→
    21→const orderSlice = createSlice({
    22→  name: 'order',
    23→  initialState,
    24→  reducers: {
    25→    saveOrderAction: saveOrder,
    26→    clearOrderAction: clearOrder,
    27→  },
    28→})
    29→
    30→// Extract the action creators object and the reducer
    31→export const { actions, reducer } = orderSlice
    32→
    33→// Extract and export each action creator by name
    34→export const { saveOrderAction, clearOrderAction } = actions
    35→
    36→// Export the reducer, either as a default or named export
    37→export default reducer
    38→

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}
    63→

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import styled from 'styled-components'
     3→
     4→import { Header, HeaderComponent } from '../components/Header/Header'
     5→import { Footer } from '../components/Footer/Footer'
     6→
     7→const Container = styled.div`
     8→  min-height: calc(100vh - 200px);
     9→`
    10→
    11→type PageTemplateProperties = {
    12→  type?: 'default' | 'sticky-header' | 'basic'
    13→}
    14→
    15→export const PageTemplate: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<PageTemplateProperties>> = ({
    16→  type = 'default',
    17→  children,
    18→}) => {
    19→  switch (type) {
    20→    case 'sticky-header': {
    21→      return (
    22→        <>
    23→          <Header sticky />
    24→          <Container>{children}</Container>
    25→          <Footer />
    26→        </>
    27→      )
    28→    }
    29→    case 'basic': {
    30→      return (
    31→        <>
    32→          <HeaderComponent logoOnly />
    33→          <Container>{children}</Container>

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→export const useKey = (key: string, keyDownCb = () => {}, keyUpCb = () => {}): boolean => {
     4→  const [pressed, setPressed] = useState(false)
     5→  const match = useCallback((event: any) => key.toLowerCase() === event.key.toLowerCase(), [key])
     6→
     7→  const onKeyDown = useCallback(
     8→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
     9→      if (match(event)) {
    10→        setPressed(true)
    11→        keyDownCb()
    12→      }
    13→    },
    14→    [keyDownCb, match]
    15→  )
    16→
    17→  const onKeyUp = useCallback(
    18→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
    19→      if (match(event)) {
    20→        setPressed(false)
    21→        keyUpCb()
    22→      }
    23→    },
    24→    [keyUpCb, match]
    25→  )
    26→
    27→  useEffect(() => {
    28→    globalThis.addEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    29→    globalThis.addEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    30→
    31→    return () => {
    32→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    33→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    34→    }
    35→  }, [key, onKeyDown, onKeyUp])
    36→
    37→  return pressed
    38→}
    39→

.storybook/
├── main.ts                        # Storybook config
├── preview.tsx                   # Storybook preview settings
└── eval-support/
    ├── transcript.tsx
    └── transcript.types.ts
```

---

### 2. ENTRY POINT & ROOT SETUP

**File:** `/src/index.tsx`
```typescript
- Creates React root and mounts App component
- Initializes service worker (unregistered by default)
```

**File:** `/src/App.tsx`
```typescript
Provider Structure (inside-out):
1. Router (BrowserRouter)
   2. StoreProvider (Redux store)
      3. ThemeProvider (styled-components - light/dark mode)
         4. GlobalStyle component
            5. AppRoutes component
```

Key features:
- Dark mode support via `use-dark-mode` hook
- Theme switching between lightTheme and darkTheme
- All providers wrap the routes

---

### 3. ROUTING & MODAL SETUP

**File:** `/src/Routes.tsx`
- Defines all app routes
- Creates `<div id="modal" />` portal element
- Routes:
  - `/` → HomePage
  - `/categories` → CategoryListPage
  - `/categories/:id` → CategoryDetailPage
  - `/restaurants/:id` → RestaurantDetailPage
  - `/checkout` → CheckoutPage
  - `/success` → SuccessPage
- Auto-scrolls to top on route changes

---

### 4. PROVIDERS & CONTEXTS

**Redux Store** (`/src/app-state/`)
- **Slices:**
  - `cart`: Manages shopping cart items and visibility
    - State: `{ visible: boolean, items: CartItem[] }`
    - Actions: toggleVisibility, saveItem, clearItem, removeItem, clearCart
  - `order`: Stores confirmed orders
    - State: `{ items: CartItem[] }`
    - Actions: saveOrder, clearOrder
- **Hooks:**
  - `useAppDispatch()` - typed dispatch
  - `useAppSelector()` - typed selector

**Theme Provider** (`/src/styles/theme.ts`)
- Exports `lightTheme` and `darkTheme`
- Theme structure:
  ```typescript
  {
    name: 'light' | 'dark'
    fonts: { family: string }
    spacing: { xxs, xs, s, m, l, xl, xxl }
    color: { 100+ color definitions }
    borderRadius: { xs, s, m, l, xl, xxl, round }
    boxShadow: { card, inner, outerBorder }
    typography: { fontSize, fontWeight }
  }
  ```

**No other Context Providers** - only Redux, Router, and Styled-Components ThemeProvider

---

### 5. GLOBAL CSS & STYLING

**GlobalStyle Component** (`/src/styles/GlobalStyle.tsx`)
- Imports external CSS:
  - `react-multi-carousel/lib/styles.css`
  - `react-loading-skeleton/dist/skeleton.css`
- Defines font-faces (Montserrat 4 weights, Hind 2 weights) from Google Fonts
- Global reset and normalization
- 250ms smooth transitions for dark mode
- `.container` class with responsive padding
- `.carousel-item` opacity animations

**CSS Reset** (`/src/styles/CSSReset.ts`)
- Position: relative by default
- Min-height: 0 for flex items
- Resets form elements (button, input, select, textarea)
- SVG overflow visible
- Removes various UA stylesheet defaults

**Breakpoints** (`/src/styles/breakpoints.ts`)
- XS: 400px
- S: 640px
- M: 768px
- L: 1024px
- XL: 1440px

---

### 6. DATA FETCHING HOOKS

**File:** `/src/api/hooks.ts`

**useFetchRestaurants()**
- Fetches all restaurants
- Returns: `{ status, restaurants, retryRequest }`

**useFetchRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId?: string)**
- Fetches restaurants filtered by category
- Returns: `{ status, restaurants, retryRequest }`

**useFetchRestaurant(id: string)**
- Fetches single restaurant by ID
- Returns: `{ status, restaurant, retryRequest }`

All hooks use status ('idle' | 'loading' | 'success' | error code) and have retry capability.

**API Service** (`/src/api/index.ts`)
- Class: `RestaurantsApi`
- Base URL: `https://mealdrop.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/restaurants`
- Simple caching in Map (skipped in test/Storybook/browser test environments)
- Methods: getRestaurants(), getRestaurantById(), getRestaurantsByCategory()

---

### 7. PORTAL & MODAL IMPLEMENTATION

**Portal Component** (`/src/components/Portal.tsx`)
```typescript
- Wrapper around React's createPortal
- Takes selector prop (e.g., "#modal")
- Used to render modals outside normal DOM hierarchy
- Returns null if portal element doesn't exist
```

**Modal Component** (`/src/components/Modal/Modal.tsx`)
```typescript
- Props: isOpen, onClose, children
- Uses Portal to render into "#modal" element
- Features:
  - CSSTransition for smooth animations (300ms)
  - Escape key support via useKey hook
  - Body scroll lock via useLockBodyScroll
  - Backdrop click to close
  - Close button in top bar
  - Two separate transitions: content + backdrop
```

**Portal Usage in Routes:**
- `<div id="modal" />` created in Routes component
- Modal rendered into this portal when opened

---

### 8. CUSTOM HOOKS

**useBodyScrollLock(shouldLock = false)** (`/src/hooks/useBodyScrollLock.ts`)
- Sets `document.body.style.overflow` to 'hidden' or 'auto'
- On mobile, also sets `height: 100vh` when locked
- Cleanup returns scroll to normal on unmount

**useKey(key, keyDownCb, keyUpCb)** (`/src/hooks/useKeyboard.ts`)
- Listens for specific keyboard key
- Returns boolean for current pressed state
- Supports callbacks for keydown/keyup
- Case-insensitive matching
- Adds/removes global event listeners

---

### 9. REUSABLE COMPONENTS

**Layout Components:**
- Header (with optional sticky/logoOnly variants)
- Footer
- Sidebar
- PageSection
- PageTemplate (wrapper with Header/Footer variants)

**UI Components:**

- IconButton
- Icon
- Badge
- Breadcrumb
- TopBanner
- ErrorBlock

**Form Components:**
- Input
- Select
- (Part of checkout multi-step form)

**Content Components:**
- RestaurantCard (with progress variants: basic, unstyled)
- Category
- Review
- AnimatedIllustration

**Data Display Components:**

- ShoppingCartMenu
- Spinner

**Typography Components:**
- Heading (level 1-4)
- Body

**Modal/Overlay:**
- Modal
- Portal
- FoodItemModal (RestaurantDetail page)

---

### 10. PAGE COMPONENTS

1. **HomePage** (`/src/pages/HomePage/`)
   - Composed of sections:
     - Banner
     - RestaurantsSection (with data-fetching container)
     - AwardWinningSection
     - CategoriesSection
   - Uses PageTemplate with default Header/Footer

2. **CategoryListPage** (`/src/pages/CategoryListPage/`)
   - Lists all categories with data

3. **CategoryDetailPage** (`/src/pages/CategoryDetailPage/`)
   - Shows restaurants in selected category

4. **RestaurantDetailPage** (`/src/pages/RestaurantDetailPage/`)
   - Menu display in sections: food, dessert, drinks
   - FoodItem components (clickable to open modal)
   - FoodItemModal for item details and add-to-cart
   - Uses modal portal for item details

5. **CheckoutPage** (`/src/pages/CheckoutPage/`)
   - MultiStepForm (using react-hook-form + zod validation)
     - ContactDetails (name, email, phone)
     - DeliveryDetails (address fields)
     - StepIndicator
   - OrderSummary sidebar
   - Custom styling with top/bottom backgrounds

6. **SuccessPage** (`/src/pages/SuccessPage/`)
   - Confirmation screen after order

---

### 11. STORYBOOK CONFIGURATION

**Main Config** (`/.storybook/main.ts`)
```typescript
{
  stories: [
    '../src/**/*.mdx',
    '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',
    './eval-support/*.mdx',
  ],
  addons: [
    '@chromatic-com/storybook',
    '@storybook/addon-vitest',
    '@storybook/addon-a11y',
    '@storybook/addon-docs',
    '@storybook/addon-onboarding',
  ],
  framework: '@storybook/react-vite',
}
```

**Preview Config** (`/.storybook/preview.tsx`)
```typescript
{
  parameters: {
    controls: {
      matchers: {
        color: /(background|color)$/i,
        date: /Date$/i,
      },
    },
    a11y: {
      test: 'todo',
    },
  },
}
```

**Storybook Scripts:**
- `storybook` - Start dev server on port 6006
- `build-storybook` - Build static version
- `vitest:storybook` - Run Storybook vitest tests
- `chromatic` - Run Chromatic visual testing

---

### 12. KEY DEPENDENCIES

**Core:**
- React 19.0.0, React DOM 19.0.0
- React Router DOM 7.2.0
- TypeScript 5.7.3

**State Management:**
- Redux Toolkit 2.6.0
- React Redux 9.2.0

**Styling:**
- Styled Components 6.1.15
- Stylis 4.3.6

**Forms:**
- React Hook Form 7.54.2
- @hookform/resolvers 4.1.3
- Zod 3.24.2

**UI Libraries:**
- React Multi Carousel 2.8.5
- React Loading Skeleton 3.5.0
- React Lottie Player 2.1.0
- React Transition Group 4.4.5

**Utilities:**
- Axios 1.8.1
- use-dark-mode 2.3.1

**Build Tools:**
- Vite 6.2.0
- Vitest 4.0.15
- Storybook (pre-release)

**Testing:**
- @testing-library/react 16.2.0
- @testing-library/dom 10.4.1
- MSW 2.7.3

---

### 13. BUILD & PACKAGE SETUP

**Package Manager:** Yarn 4.2.1 (with resolutions for patch)

**Scripts:**
```json
{
  "start": "vite",
  "build": "tsc && vite build",
  "serve": "vite preview",
  "check": "tsc --noEmit",
  "test": "vitest",
  "test:ci": "vitest run",
  "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage",
  "storybook": "storybook dev -p 6006",
  "build-storybook": "storybook build",
  "vitest:storybook": "vitest run --project=storybook",
  "lint": "eslint --fix .",
  "prettier": "prettier --write .",
  "pre-commit": "lint-staged",
  "chromatic": "chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes --only-changed"
}
```

**MSW Setup:** Service Worker directory at `/public`

---

### 14. TYPE DEFINITIONS

**File:** `/src/types/index.ts`

```typescript
export type ShopItem = {
  id: number
  name: string
  price: number
  imageUrl?: string
  description?: string
}

export type FoodMenuItem = {
  id: number
  name: string
  description?: string
  price: number
}

export type Restaurant = {
  name: string
  id?: string
  mapsUrl?: string
  rating?: number
  url?: string
  address?: string
  specialty: string
  photoUrl: string
  isClosed?: boolean
  categories?: string[]
  isLoading?: boolean
  isNew?: boolean
  menu: {
    food: FoodMenuItem[]
    dessert: FoodMenuItem[]
    drinks: FoodMenuItem[]
  }
}

export type CartItem = ShopItem & { quantity: number }
```

---

## SUMMARY

This is a **restaurant ordering application** built with modern React practices:

- **Architecture:** Redux for global state (cart, orders), styled-components for styling, React Router for navigation
- **Data Flow:** API calls → hooks → Redux → Components
- **Styling:** Theme-based with light/dark mode support, responsive design with breakpoints
- **Modals:** Portal-based modal system using React 18's createPortal
- **Forms:** Multi-step checkout using react-hook-form + Zod validation
- **Testing:** Vitest + Testing Library setup with Storybook integration
- **Key Features:** Shopping cart, multi-category restaurant browsing, detailed item views with modals, checkout flow, order confirmation

All files are organized by feature/layer with clear separation of concerns. No legacy Context API usage—all global state via Redux.

    },
  },
}
```

**Storybook Scripts:**
- `storybook` - Start dev server on port 6006
- `build-storybook` - Build static version
- `vitest:storybook` - Run Storybook vitest tests
- `chromatic` - Run Chromatic visual testing

---

### 12. KEY DEPENDENCIES

**Core:**
- React 19.0.0, React DOM 19.0.0
- React Router DOM 7.2.0
- TypeScript 5.7.3

**State Management:**
- Redux Toolkit 2.6.0
- React Redux 9.2.0

**Styling:**
- Styled Components 6.1.15
- Stylis 4.3.6

**Forms:**
- React Hook Form 7.54.2
- @hookform/resolvers 4.1.3
- Zod 3.24.2

**UI Libraries:**
- React Multi Carousel 2.8.5
- React Loading Skeleton 3.5.0
- React Lottie Player 2.1.0
- React Transition Group 4.4.5

**Utilities:**
- Axios 1.8.1
- use-dark-mode 2.3.1

**Build Tools:**
- Vite 6.2.0
- Vitest 4.0.15
- Storybook (pre-release)

**Testing:**
- @testing-library/react 16.2.0
- @testing-library/dom 10.4.1
- MSW 2.7.3

---

### 13. BUILD & PACKAGE SETUP

**Package Manager:** Yarn 4.2.1 (with resolutions for patch)

**Scripts:**
```json
{
  "start": "vite",
  "build": "tsc && vite build",
  "serve": "vite preview",
  "check": "tsc --noEmit",
  "test": "vitest",
  "test:ci": "vitest run",
  "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage",
  "storybook": "storybook dev -p 6006",
  "build-storybook": "storybook build",
  "vitest:storybook": "vitest run --project=storybook",
  "lint": "eslint --fix .",
  "prettier": "prettier --write .",
  "pre-commit": "lint-staged",
  "chromatic": "chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes --only-changed"
}
```

**MSW Setup:** Service Worker directory at `/public`

---

### 14. TYPE DEFINITIONS

**File:** `/src/types/index.ts`

```typescript
export type ShopItem = {
  id: number
  name: string
  price: number
  imageUrl?: string
  description?: string
}

export type FoodMenuItem = {
  id: number
  name: string
  description?: string
  price: number
}

export type Restaurant = {
  name: string
  id?: string
  mapsUrl?: string
  rating?: number
  url?: string
  address?: string
  specialty: string
  photoUrl: string
  isClosed?: boolean
  categories?: string[]
  isLoading?: boolean
  isNew?: boolean
  menu: {
    food: FoodMenuItem[]
    dessert: FoodMenuItem[]
    drinks: FoodMenuItem[]
  }
}

export type CartItem = ShopItem & { quantity: number }
```

---

## SUMMARY

This is a **restaurant ordering application** built with modern React practices:

- **Architecture:** Redux for global state (cart, orders), styled-components for styling, React Router for navigation
- **Data Flow:** API calls → hooks → Redux → Components
- **Styling:** Theme-based with light/dark mode support, responsive design with breakpoints
- **Modals:** Portal-based modal system using React 18's createPortal
- **Forms:** Multi-step checkout using react-hook-form + Zod validation
- **Testing:** Vitest + Testing Library setup with Storybook integration
- **Key Features:** Shopping cart, multi-category restaurant browsing, detailed item views with modals, checkout flow, order confirmation

All files are organized by feature/layer with clear separation of concerns. No legacy Context API usage—all global state via Redux.

import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

import { DefaultTheme } from 'styled-components'

declare module 'styled-components' {
  export interface DefaultTheme {
    name: 'light' | 'dark'
    fonts: {
      family: string
    }
    spacing: typeof spacing
    color: typeof color
    borderRadius: typeof borderRadius
    boxShadow: typeof boxShadow
    typography: typeof typography
  }
}

const baseColors = {
  white: '#FFFFFF ',
  black: '#202020',
  red: {
    base: '#D70808',
    light1: '#E57373',
    light2: '#EF9A9A',
    dark1: '#C62828',
    dark2: '#B71C1C',
  },
  otherBlack: '#2C2C2C ',
  grey: {
    base: '#909090',
    light1: '#A6A6A6',
    light2: '#BCBCBC',
    light3: '#D2D2D2',
    light4: '#E9E9E9',
    light5: '#F5F6F7',
    light6: '#F9F9F9',
    dark1: '#797979',
    dark2: '#636363',
    dark3: '#4D4D4D',
    dark4: '#363636',
    dark5: '#2C2C2C',
    dark6: '#202020',
  },
  green: {
    base: '#E5F8BC',
    light1: '#E8F9C3',
    light2: '#EAF9C9',
    light3: '#EDFAD0',
    light4: '#EFFBD7',
    light5: '#F2FCDE',
    dark1: '#CEDFA9',
    dark2: '#B7C696',
    dark3: '#A0AE84',
    dark4: '#899571',
    dark5: '#737C5E',
  },
  blue: {
    base: '#B1DDE4',
    light1: '#B9E0E7',
    light2: '#C1E4E9',
    light3: '#C8E7EC',
    light4: '#D0EBEF',
    light5: '#D8EEF2',
    dark1: '#9FC7CD',
    dark2: '#8EB1B6',
    dark3: '#7C9BA0',
    dark4: '#6A8589',
    dark5: '#596f72',
  },
}

const spaceUnit = 1

const borderRadius = {
  xs: '4px',
  s: '8px',
  m: '16px',
  l: '24px',
  xl: '32px',
  xxl: '40px',
  round: '50%',
}

const spacing = {
  xxs: `${0.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xs: `${0.5 * spaceUnit}em`,
  s: `${spaceUnit}em`,
  m: `${1.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  l: `${2 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xl: `${3.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xxl: `${5.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
}

const boxShadow = {
  card: '0px 14px 26px 0px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08)',
  inner: 'inset 0 3px 0 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.05)',
  outerBorder: `0 0 0 1px ${baseColors.blue.dark2}, 0 0 0 4px ${baseColors.blue.light5}`,
}

const typography = {
  fontSize: {
    body: '1.125rem',
    bodyS: '1rem',
    bodyXS: '0.9rem',
    bodyXXS: '0.72rem',
    heading1: '2.74rem',
    heading2: '2.19rem',
    heading3: '1.75rem',
    heading4: '1.4rem',
  },
  fontWeight: {
    black: '900',
    bold: '700',
    medium: '500',
    regular: '400',
  },
}

const color = {
  accentText: baseColors.black,
  badgeBackground: baseColors.grey.light4,
  badgeText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
  cartButtonText: baseColors.grey.base,
  bannerBackground: baseColors.blue.base,
  buttonClear: 'transparent',
  buttonClearHover: baseColors.grey.light5,
  buttonPrimary: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  buttonPrimaryHover: baseColors.grey.dark5, // recheck
  buttonSecondary: baseColors.green.base,
  buttonSecondaryHover: baseColors.green.dark1, // recheck
  buttonText: baseColors.white,
  cardBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  checkoutTopBackground: baseColors.green.base,
  checkoutBottomBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  error: baseColors.red.dark2,
  formBackground: baseColors.white,
  foodItemBackground: baseColors.white,
  footerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
  headerBackground: baseColors.white,
  restaurantDetailBackground: baseColors.white,
  headerBorder: baseColors.grey.light4,
  inputBackground: baseColors.grey.light5,
  inputHint: baseColors.grey.dark1,
  inputIcon: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  label: baseColors.grey.dark4,
  labelActive: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  menuSectionBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  stepsIndicatorInnerBar: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  stepsIndicatorOuterBar: baseColors.grey.light5,
  orderSummaryBackground: baseColors.white,
  overlayBackground: baseColors.white,
  overlayHeader: baseColors.grey.light5,
  primaryText: baseColors.otherBlack,
  secondaryText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
  // This is just used to demo a11y violations, use it in the Review component
  bannerText: baseColors.blue.dark3,
  reviewText: baseColors.blue.dark5,
  screenBackground: baseColors.white,
  sidebarHeader: baseColors.grey.dark4,
  sidebarFooter: baseColors.white,
  skeletonBase: baseColors.grey.light4,
  skeletonHighlight: baseColors.grey.light5,
  topBannerBackground: baseColors.green.base,
  newRestaurantTag: baseColors.green.base,
  white: baseColors.white,
  black: baseColors.black,
}

export const lightTheme: DefaultTheme = {
  borderRadius,
  boxShadow,
  color,
  fonts: {
    family: 'NunitoSans, sans-serif',
  },
  name: 'light',
  spacing,
  typography,
}

export const darkTheme: DefaultTheme = {
  ...lightTheme,
  boxShadow: {
    ...boxShadow,
    outerBorder: `0 0 0 2px ${baseColors.blue.dark5}, 0 0 0 4px ${baseColors.blue.base}`,
  },
  color: {

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→
    38→export const useFetchRestaurantsByCategory = (categoryId?: string) => {
    39→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {
    54→          setStatus('success')
    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}
    70→
    71→export const useFetchRestaurant = (id: string) => {
    72→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    73→  const [restaurant, setRestaurant] = useState<Restaurant>()
    74→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    75→
    76→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    77→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    78→  }, [setRetry])
    79→
    80→  useEffect(() => {
    81→    setStatus('loading')
    82→
    83→    api
    84→      .getRestaurantById(id)
    85→      .then((res) => {
    86→        setStatus('success')
    87→        setRestaurant(res)
    88→      })
    89→      .catch((error) => {
    90→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    91→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    92→      })
    93→  }, [id, retry])
    94→
    95→  return {
    96→    status,
    97→    restaurant,
    98→    retryRequest,
    99→  }
   100→}
   101→

1→import { combineReducers, configureStore } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'
     2→
     3→import { reducer as cart } from './cart'
     4→import { reducer as order } from './order'
     5→
     6→export const rootReducer = combineReducers({
     7→  cart,
     8→  order,
     9→})
    10→
    11→export const store = configureStore({
    12→  reducer: rootReducer,
    13→})
    14→
    15→export type AppDispatch = typeof store.dispatch
    16→export type RootState = ReturnType<typeof store.getState>
    17→

import styled from 'styled-components'

import { PageTemplate } from '../../templates/PageTemplate'
import { categories } from '../../stub/categories'

import { RestaurantsSection } from './components/RestaurantsSection'
import { CategoriesSection } from './components/CategoriesSection/CategoriesSection'
import { Banner } from './components/Banner'
import { AwardWinningSection } from './components/AwardWinningSection'

const Spacing = styled.div`
  margin-bottom: 4.5rem;
`

export const HomePage = () => (
  <PageTemplate>
    <Banner />
    <Spacing />
    <RestaurantsSection title="Our favorite picks" />
    <Spacing />
    <AwardWinningSection />
    <Spacing />
    <CategoriesSection categories={categories} />
    <Spacing />
  </PageTemplate>
)

1→import styled from 'styled-components'
     2→import { useNavigate } from 'react-router-dom'
     3→import Carousel, { type ArrowProps, type CarouselProps } from 'react-multi-carousel'
     4→
     5→import { useFetchRestaurants } from '../../../../api/hooks'
     6→import { IconButton } from '../../../../components/IconButton'
     7→import { PageSection } from '../../../../components/PageSection'
     8→import { RestaurantCard, RestaurantCardSkeleton } from '../../../../components/RestaurantCard'
     9→import { Restaurant } from '../../../../types'
    10→import { isMobile } from '../../../../helpers'
    11→
    12→const PreviousButton = styled(IconButton)`
    13→  position: absolute;
    14→  left: 0;
    15→`
    16→
    17→const NextButton = styled(IconButton)`
    18→  position: absolute;
    19→  right: 0;
    20→`
    21→
    22→type RestaurantsSectionProps = {
    23→  title: string
    24→}
    25→
    26→const CustomArrow = (props: Partial<CarouselProps & ArrowProps> & { isNext?: boolean }) => {
    27→  // filter out unnecessary props coming from react-multi-carousel
    28→  // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars
    29→  const { carouselState, rtl, isNext, ...rest } = props
    30→  return isNext ? (
    31→    <NextButton name="arrow-right" {...rest} />
    32→  ) : (
    33→    <PreviousButton name="arrow-left" {...rest} />
    34→  )
    35→}
    36→
    37→export const RestaurantsSection = ({ title }: RestaurantsSectionProps) => {
    38→  const navigate = useNavigate()
    39→
    40→  const { restaurants, status } = useFetchRestaurants()
    41→
    42→  const isOnMobile = isMobile()
    43→  return (
    44→    <PageSection title={title}>
    45→      <Carousel
    46→        draggable={isOnMobile}
    47→        partialVisible={isOnMobile}
    48→        customLeftArrow={<CustomArrow />}
    49→        customRightArrow={<CustomArrow isNext />}
    50→        responsive={{
    51→          desktop: {
    52→            breakpoint: { max: 5000, min: 1024 },
    53→            items: 3,
    54→            slidesToSlide: 3,
    55→          },
    56→          tablet: {
    57→            breakpoint: { max: 1024, min: 464 },
    58→            items: 2,
    59→            paritialVisibilityGutter: 50,
    60→          },
    61→          mobile: {
    62→            breakpoint: { max: 464, min: 0 },
    63→            items: 1,
    64→            paritialVisibilityGutter: 30,
    65→          },
    66→        }}
    67→        containerClass="carousel-container"
    68→        removeArrowOnDeviceType={['tablet', 'mobile']}
    69→        itemClass="carousel-item"
    70→      >
    71→        {status === 'loading'
    72→          ? Array.from({ length: 3 }).map((_, index) => <RestaurantCardSkeleton key={index} />)
    73→          : restaurants.map((restaurant: Restaurant, index: number) => (
    74→              <RestaurantCard
    75→                key={restaurant.name + index}
    76→                {...restaurant}
    77→                onClick={() => navigate(`/restaurants/${restaurant.id}`)}
    78→              />
    79→            ))}
    80→      </Carousel>
    81→    </PageSection>
    82→  )
    83→}
    84→

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { useKey, useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks'
     5→import { Button } from '../Button'
     6→import { Heading } from '../typography'
     7→
     8→import { SidebarContainer, SidebarContent, SidebarFooter, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Sidebar.styles'
     9→
    10→type SidebarProps = {
    11→  isOpen: boolean
    12→  title: string
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→  footer?: React.ReactNode
    15→}
    16→
    17→export const Sidebar: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<SidebarProps>> = ({
    18→  children,
    19→  footer,
    20→  isOpen,
    21→  title,
    22→  onClose,
    23→}) => {
    24→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    25→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    26→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    27→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    28→
    29→  return (
    30→    <>
    31→      <CSSTransition
    32→        in={isOpen}
    33→        timeout={300}
    34→        classNames="sidebar"
    35→        unmountOnExit
    36→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    37→      >
    38→        <SidebarContainer data-testid="sidebar" ref={contentRef}>
    39→          <TopBar>
    40→            <Heading level={4}>{title}</Heading>
    41→            <Button
    42→              aria-label="close sidebar"
    43→              data-testid="sidebar-close-btn"
    44→              onClick={onClose}
    45→              clear
    46→              round
    47→              icon="cross"
    48→              iconSize={16}
    49→            />
    50→          </TopBar>
    51→          <SidebarContent data-testid="sidebar-content">{children}</SidebarContent>
    52→          {footer && <SidebarFooter data-testid="sidebar-footer">{footer}</SidebarFooter>}
    53→        </SidebarContainer>
    54→      </CSSTransition>
    55→
    56→      <CSSTransition
    57→        in={isOpen}
    58→        timeout={300}
    59→        classNames="backdrop"
    60→        unmountOnExit
    61→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    62→      >
    63→        <Backdrop data-testid="Sidebar-backdrop" onClick={onClose} />
    64→      </CSSTransition>
    65→    </>
    66→  )
    67→}
    68→

  "scripts": {
    "build": "tsc && vite build",

content.content:
1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}
    63→

content.thinking:
The HomePage test is failing for the `Default` story - it can't find the restaurant card after waiting 5 seconds. The `LoadingState` story passes though. Let me check what's happening more carefully. 

Looking at the error more carefully, it seems like the MSW is possibly not working or the data is not being returned. Let me check - the API base URL is `https://mealdrop.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/restaurants` and the MSW handler should be intercepting requests to this URL.

Wait, but the `RestaurantsSection` component uses `useFetchRestaurants` which calls `api.getRestaurants()` which uses `axios`. 

MSW does work with axios in browser mode, so the issue might be that the test is still showing a loading spinner because the network request is being intercepted but maybe not resolving properly. Looking at the error output, it seems to be showing some HTML content that I need to examine more closely to understand what's actually being rendered.

Interesting! The MSW is intercepting the request and returning data (200 with restaurants), but the test is still timing out waiting for `restaurant-card`. Let me look more carefully...

Looking at the output, the request is made and the response comes back with data. So MSW is working. But the test timed out waiting for `restaurant-card`.

The issue is that the `RestaurantsSection` uses a carousel (`react-multi-carousel`). The cards might render but not be accessible via `getByTestId`. Let me check if there's something about the carousel that wraps the items.


Actually, I see the problem now. The `RestaurantsSection` conditionally renders either skeleton loaders while loading or the actual restaurant cards once data arrives. The hook that fetches the restaurants has logic to show skeletons during the loading state and then swap them out for the real cards when the status changes to success.

But wait, the MSW mock is returning a successful 200 response with data, so the status should definitely be changing. Looking at the test output more carefully, I can see HTML content is actually being rendered with the right structure. The issue might be that the carousel only displays visible items, so `getAllByTestId` can't find cards that are outside the viewport. Or possibly the cards are rendering outside the canvas element entirely in the Storybook setup, which would explain why the test can't locate them even though the data loaded successfully. the carousel component (react-multi-carousel) has issues.

Let me look at what the canvas finds after the timeout...

The error shows the HTML but I can't see all of it. Let me try a different approach - get the full HTML output to understand what's rendered.

Actually, I suspect the issue might be that `restaurant-card` is rendered but it's not visible because the carousel may render cards as hidden/offscreen. Let me check if `react-multi-carousel` applies `aria-hidden` or similar to items that are not active.

Looking at the RestaurantsSection code, the carousel renders all items but might add visibility styles. Let me try `getAllByTestId` instead and check if the elements exist at all, regardless of visibility.

So the real question is whether the data is even triggering a re-render - the request succeeded with a 200 response and MSW handled it correctly, but `waitFor` timed out waiting for the cards to appear. Either the carousel isn't rendering them, they're hidden from the visibility check, or there's some state issue I'm missing. Let me try checking for text content instead of relying on the testid and visibility assertion. The carousel might be cloning elements and duplicating the test IDs, or maybe react-multi-carousel needs a specific viewport size to render properly in tests. I should also check if the shared store singleton is causing state to leak between stories. Let me make the test more resilient by checking for restaurant names with `getByText` instead, or using `getAllByTestId` to verify at least one item exists.

content.thinking:
The RestaurantDetailPage tests are failing with a Router `invariant` error. This is because we're nesting `MemoryRouter` inside the global `MemoryRouter` from the preview decorator, and React Router 7 doesn't support nested routers.

I need to fix this. The solution is to not include the global MemoryRouter for this story and instead let the story decorator handle the router.

To handle this in Storybook, I can use the story's `decorators` to replace the global decorator. But decorators in Storybook stack, they don't replace each other.
 
I'm weighing two main approaches: either remove the global router entirely and add it only to stories that need it, or keep the global router but find a way to override it for specific stories. The first option would require adding routers to many component stories since Header and ShoppingCartMenu both depend on routing, but it gives me more control. The second option is cleaner globally but requires a more complex conditional setup.

Looking at the actual error from React Router 7, it seems the issue is that nesting routers creates a conflict with the context system, whereas in React Router 6 this was supposed to work with each router maintaining its own context.

So I need to rethink this. Instead of nesting routers, I could configure the global decorator to read story-level parameters and use those to set up the initial route and matching rules dynamically. This way, the RestaurantDetailPage story can specify what route it needs through parameters, and the decorator handles setting up the MemoryRouter with the right configuration.

Setting up the decorator logic... Setting up the decorator to accept route configuration through parameters so each story can specify its own initial entries and path pattern for the Routes component. I'm settling on using parameters for routing configuration instead of decorators, which will let the global decorator handle the MemoryRouter setup consistently across all stories. I'll update both the preview file and the RestaurantDetailPage stories to implement this cleaner approach.

content.thinking:
The TypeScript errors are about missing `args` in story objects. When using `StoryObj<typeof meta>` with a `render` function, Storybook normally doesn't require `args` since the render function provides all the props. However, with strict TypeScript, it seems that when all props are required (no optional), the story type requires `args`.

The issue is that `StoryObj<typeof meta>` when the component has required props, TypeScript enforces `args` to be provided. But when we use `render` functions, we typically don't need `args`.

The fix is to either:
1. Use `Partial` args type at the meta level: `satisfies Meta<Partial<typeof Component>>`


2. Or provide `args: {}` in the meta default and make all required fields have defaults
3. Or provide `args` in each story

Looking at the Storybook docs, the pattern for stories with a `render` function that doesn't use args is to use `type Story = StoryObj<typeof meta>` without args. But when the component has required props, TypeScript complains. The simplest fix is to add a cast on the meta to make the args optional, or just provide `args: {}` in the meta itself.

Wait, the error is that `StoryObj<typeof meta>` infers the args as required based on the component's required props. The best approach is to either remove the `satisfies Meta<typeof Component>` constraint and set the component directly, or add args defaults in meta. Let me check how the existing stories in `src/stories/` handle this—looking at the Button.stories file, they're using a setup that includes default args like `onClick: fn()` in the meta definition.

For the Badge component, since we're using a custom `render` function instead of relying on args, the issue is that TypeScript still expects `args` to be present in the story type. The simplest solution is to add an empty `args: {}` in the meta, which satisfies the type requirement while letting the render function handle the actual component rendering. the real issue is that `satisfies Meta<typeof Badge>` forces TypeScript to require the `text` field in `args` for every story, even when using `render` functions. The fix is to provide default `args` in the meta with a value like `{ text: 'burgers' }`, which lets stories override it as needed, or switch to using `args` instead of `render` in the stories themselves. I'm trying to understand how Storybook's type inference actually works here to find the cleanest solution. Setting default args in the meta object for each component... I'm settling on providing minimal args in the meta for the stories that need them—Badge requires `text`, RestaurantCard needs `name`, `specialty`, and `photoUrl`, and ShoppingCartMenu needs `isOpen`, `totalPrice`, `onClose`, `cartItems`, and `onItemChange`. Now I need to check if the other components like Button, Header, TopBanner, and Review have similar TypeScript issues.

content.text: The TypeScript errors are about missing `args` when component has required props. I'll fix by adding default `args` to the affected metas:

Here's a summary of everything set up:

**Storybook environment (`/.storybook/`):**
- `preview.tsx` — wraps stories with `MemoryRouter` (routing via `parameters.router`), Redux `StoreProvider`, `ThemeProvider` (light theme), `GlobalStyle`, and MSW via `msw-storybook-addon`
- `msw-handlers.ts` — intercepts all 3 API endpoints (`GET /restaurants`, `?id=`, `?category=`) returning stub data
- `main.ts` — added `staticDirs: ['../public']` so `mockServiceWorker.js` is served
- `preview-body.html` — adds `<div id="modal">` for the portal-based modal

**10 story files written (40 tests, all passing):**
- `Button.stories.tsx` — 5 stories (Primary, Clear, Disabled, WithIcon, Large)
- `Badge.stories.tsx` — 3 stories
- `Review.stories.tsx` — 3 stories (NoRating, VeryGood, Excellent)
- `RestaurantCard.stories.tsx` — 4 stories (Default, Closed, New, Loading skeleton)
- `TopBanner.stories.tsx` — 3 stories
- `Header.stories.tsx` — 3 stories (Default, WithCartTotal, LogoOnly)
- `ShoppingCartMenu.stories.tsx` — 3 stories (Open, Empty, Closed)
- `HomePage.stories.tsx` — 2 stories (waits for MSW data to load)
- `RestaurantDetailPage.stories.tsx` — 3 stories (uses `parameters.router` for `/restaurants/:id`)
- `CheckoutPage.stories.tsx` — 3 stories (including multi-step form interaction)

providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import type { Preview } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ const preview: Preview = { │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - │
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ const preview: Preview = { │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }; │ export default preview; │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = { │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import type { Meta, StoryObj } from '@storybook/react-vite'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = { │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ } satisfies Meta; │ export default meta; │ type Story = StoryObj; │ export const Default: Story = { │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
     2→import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
     3→import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
     4→import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'
     5→
     6→import { store } from './app-state'
     7→import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
     8→import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
     9→import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'
    10→
    11→export default function App() {
    12→  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
    13→  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme
    14→
    15→  return (
    16→    <Router>
    17→      <StoreProvider store={store}>
    18→        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    19→          <GlobalStyle />
    20→          <AppRoutes />
    21→        </ThemeProvider>
    22→      </StoreProvider>
    23→    </Router>
    24→  )
    25→}

1→import { useLayoutEffect } from 'react'
     2→import { Routes, Route, useLocation } from 'react-router-dom'
     3→
     4→import { CategoryListPage } from './pages/CategoryListPage'
     5→import { CategoryDetailPage } from './pages/CategoryDetailPage'
     6→import { RestaurantDetailPage } from './pages/RestaurantDetailPage'
     7→import { HomePage } from './pages/HomePage'
     8→import { CheckoutPage } from './pages/CheckoutPage'
     9→import { SuccessPage } from './pages/SuccessPage'
    10→
    11→export const AppRoutes: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<unknown>> = ({ children }) => {
    12→  const location = useLocation()
    13→  // Scroll to top when a path changes
    14→  useLayoutEffect(() => {
    15→    window.scrollTo(0, 0)
    16→  }, [location.pathname])
    17→
    18→  return (
    19→    <>
    20→      <div id="modal" />
    21→      <Routes>
    22→        <Route path="/categories" element={<CategoryListPage />} />
    23→        <Route path="/categories/:id" element={<CategoryDetailPage />} />
    24→        <Route path="/restaurants/:id" element={<RestaurantDetailPage />} />
    25→        <Route path="/checkout" element={<CheckoutPage />} />
    26→        <Route path="/success" element={<SuccessPage />} />
    27→        <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
    28→        {children}
    29→      </Routes>
    30→    </>
    31→  )
    32→}
